6	
7	## Cisco Systems (Cloud Networking Group)
8	
9	### Anand Singh
10	
11	- **Email:** anandksi@cisco.com
12	- **Title:** Senior Director, Cloud Networking Group
13	- **Role in Engagement:** Executive sponsor. Escalation point. Budget authority (~$100K/quarter). Created the original WebEx group on Feb 10 and directed the team to engage with Colin immediately.
14	- **Ownership:** Budget allocation, team assignments, executive oversight. Can involve VP-level (Arun) when needed. Controls onboarding escalations -- added Shih-Ta Chi on Mar 26 specifically to unblock onboarding.
15	- **Sentiment:** Supportive. On Apr 16, granted BayOne a full next-quarter contract extension without requiring a formal plan-in-hand. Said the team is "doing great" and would have signed a full-year extension, but held to three months in light of outstanding access issues. This signals patience is intact and confidence in the team is high despite the slow pace of Cisco-side access provisioning.
16	- **Prior sentiment progression (pre-extension):** Patience was thinning across March. His WebEx messages got progressively shorter and more direct:
17	  - Mar 3: "How is it going? Is everyone on board?"
18	  - Mar 12: "Do you have any visibility? Anywhere I can help? This is losing steam."
19	  - Mar 23: "What are next steps? Is all access/permission taken care?"
20	  - Mar 27: "Can we come up with a tentative plan for next couple of weeks?"
21	  - Mar 31: "Any update on this?"
22	  He was asking for a PLAN, not status updates. The Apr 16 extension suggests he now has enough confidence to extend runway rather than demand a plan.
23	- **Working Style:** Direct communicator. Checks in periodically but does not micromanage. Offered to help unblock procurement and access issues when escalated to. Monitors the WebEx space and will expedite if things are not moving. In the Feb 17 meeting, said he would monitor the WebEx group and intervene if questions go unanswered. Told Colin to keep him looped and ping him if anything stalls. Wants recorded sessions so onboarding scales. Understands "business reality versus engineering reality" -- flexible on quarter boundaries.
24	- **Sentiment update (2026-04-20, Team Set 06d — damage control call):** Joined the prep conversation with Colin and Namita unexpectedly the morning of the CSIRT incident, and delivered the best possible outcome from the Cisco executive layer on day-of. Volunteered to be the Cisco-side point of contact for the GPS review. Endorsed the scope-correctness of the WebEx scraper work. Accepted the Namita access suspension as appropriate. Framed the incident as recoverable by citing precedent with Cisco full-time employees: "it has happened for full-time employees also sometime. So it should be, I hope it should be okay, but let them go through their finding. Whatever recommendation they come up, of course we'll comply with that. And anything I have to justify, I'll justify that as well." Committed to chasing Namita's access restoration timeline after findings clear. Engagement forward-progress frame explicitly preserved: "I just want to make sure it gets quickly resolved so we continue to make progress." This behavior confirms the Apr 16 "full confidence" posture survived the incident at the executive-sponsor layer.
25	- **Known picture vs. full CSIRT record (2026-04-20):** Anand's picture of the incident is narrower than what Matt Healy has on file. Colin disclosed the four Python files, the Teams channel error, and the Namita access suspension. Colin did not surface the 26.77 GB zip and 80.7 GB total data figures, the BayOne-to-BayOne Teams tenant path over Cisco hardware, the BayOne GitHub upload of Log_type_mapping.pdf, the AirDrop of the presentation document, or the factual gap about whether Colin knew of the GitHub upload before Namita's disclosure. If GPS briefs Anand with the full scope later, this gap will be visible. Colin's posture is defensible as normal executive briefing practice, but the exposure remains.
26	- **Known Since:** Nov 2025 (first conversations about CI/CD opportunity); Feb 10, 2026 (created engagement WebEx group)
27	
28	---
29	
30	### Srinivas Pitta ("Srini")
31	
32	- **Email:** spitta@cisco.com
33	- **Title:** Director of Engineering / AI Lead, Cloud Networking Group
34	- **Role in Engagement:** Technical visionary. Owns the DeepSight Atlas platform. Defines what BayOne builds and how it integrates. Will be BayOne's primary technical counterpart once development begins.
35	- **Ownership:** DeepSight Atlas platform (Triage app live, Runbook launching, CI/CD app in progress). AI infrastructure, SDK, AI stack. Repo creation for new DeepSight applications. Technical strategy for agentic infrastructure and extensibility.
36	- **Sentiment:** Enthusiastic, direct, fast-paced. Treats partners as colleagues, not vendors. Told Colin in Feb 17 meeting: "Once you are onboarding, you are my friend. So I'll treat you the way -- treat me the same way as a colleague." Expects candor and pushback -- "you have to correct me" and "feel good to correct me." Shared DeepSight recording and platform access proactively. Expressed "full trust and confidence" in Colin specifically. Self-aware about his intensity: "sometimes team might say, why Srini is so aggressive, right? Because that's my nature."
37	- **Sentiment update (2026-04-20, Team Set 06):** On the morning of 2026-04-20, jointly with Anand Singh, called a 15-minute meeting with Colin to address the Cisco IT Security incident involving Namita's data-handling violations. The fact that Srinivas personally called this meeting rather than routing it through Matt Healy or handling it via chat represents the first executive-level friction event in the engagement. It is a material change from the Feb 17 "full trust and confidence" posture and the Apr 16 renewal-with-confidence posture. The 15-minute duration is mildly positive — indicates intent to address directly and move forward rather than open an extended review — but the escalation to a co-meeting with Anand means the trust posture is now being tested rather than assumed. Post-meeting, this entry must be updated again based on the tone and outcome of the conversation.
38	- **Working Style:** Moves very fast. Values extensibility over point solutions -- always wants two hats: "current need and future need." Thinks in terms of agentic infrastructure. Will pull BayOne into broader meetings once development starts so they have context for future phases. Expects an app running live within two months of onboarding because "all the infrastructure is already built for you." Collaborative -- will include BayOne in heated technical discussions as equals.
39	- **Known Since:** Feb 17, 2026 (in-person discovery meeting)

## Bridge Document Question

This is the one wrinkle: **Set 07's source date (Apr 13) is chronologically BEFORE Sets 01–06g** (which span Apr 10 → Apr 21). The team chain was built forward from Apr 10, and we're now inserting a Set whose source predates most of it.

Three options:
1. **Write `06g-07_changes_2026-04-13.md`** — bridge from last-processed (06g) to newly-processed (07), explaining it's a retroactive gap-fill and what this earlier meeting tells us that the later sets already captured/missed

3. **Skip the bridge entirely** — this set predates the others and doesn't naturally connect (bridges are retrospective per the methodology)

My recommendation: **Option 1** — a `06g-07` bridge, explicitly framed as "retroactive gap fill." Maintains the chain continuity and is honest about the processing order vs. source order.

Covered Srinivas debrief, tool access, architecture principles, and weekly deliverables. |\n| Namita Ravikiran Mane | Agentic AI / Airflow Specialist, BayOne (on-site San Jose, build log track lead) | Full | Quiet but substantive. Confirmed ADS = Aurora Development Server (not Active Directory). Surfaced the CI versus CD distinction from the Friday call and said she would focus on the CD (nightly builds) gap. |\n| Saurav Kumar Mishra | AI/ML Engineer, BayOne (offshore India, WebEx scraper and bot lead) | Full (most vocal offshore contributor) | Drove most of the technical depth in the call alongside Colin. Brought up Cisco IT flagging his Wall-E bot as non-compliant, the WebEx developer platform taxonomy (integrations, service apps, bots, agentic apps, OAuth), and cost-per-request scale math. Reused the Volley-bot Podman work as working proof. |\n| Srikar Madarapu | AI Engineer, BayOne (on-site San Jose, WebEx scraper track lead) | Moderate | Clarified key facts when asked. Corrected Saurav on GitHub Desktop versus Docker Desktop install confusion. Confirmed Scribble is Naga-owned (not Justin-owned) and is a local script, not an integrated system. Shared A2G access state (granted but not visible, same as Saurav). |\n| Vaishali Sonawane | BayOne (onboarding, log-side partner to Namita) | Silent observer | Single utterance in the meeting (\"Thank you, everyone\" at close). Colin opened the call confirming she was present and noted he had just finished a separate one-on-one with her. Still waiting on Cisco hardware, so she is scoped out of access requests this week. Paired with Namita on the log-side track. |\n\n---\n\n## Team Dynamics Observed\n\n### Colin as project lead and senior engineer\n\nColin was clearly in the role of orchestrator and senior engineer. He ran the meeting, debriefed the Apr 10 Srinivas call, set the week's priorities, and walked through three substantial technical tutorials unprompted:\n\n1. How to think about Podman versus Docker at Cisco, including the licensing backstory for why Cisco defaults to Podman.\n2. What ADS machines actually are (Aurora Development Server, virtual machines likely running on something Proxmox-class, Linux so concurrent multi-user access is possible).\n3. How to do proper A/B testing for the Scribble versus WebEx transcription comparison, framed as a three-tier decomposition: raw quality, glossary-augmented quality, then compute and scale cost.\n\nColin also set expectations about diplomatic framing when pushing back on Cisco-side work quality. He explicitly told the team not to call Naga's Scribble work junk in writing; instead, present current-state and proposed-state architecture diagrams and let technical stakeholders draw their own conclusions. He said literally, \"you can't get your feathers ruffled if it's not really working for real yet.\"\n\n### Saurav as the strongest offshore contributor\n\nSaurav carried most of the technical back-and-forth. He brought in real observational data about the Cisco WebEx developer portal (the integrations, service apps, bots, agentic apps, OAuth taxonomy), flagged the compliance issue with his Wall-E bot being flagged by Cisco IT, and proposed a decoupled architecture (scraper, DB, MCP, then bot or app on top) before Colin arrived at the same framing. He also caught the economic logic about compute cost per transcript with characteristic bluntness: \"if we can save like suppose 50 cents on each request, I suppose there will be millions coming in a month.\"\n\n### Namita carrying the onboarding torch\n\nNamita was the only person in the meeting who had actually gotten GitHub Enterprise access working. She followed the PDF she had shared with the team on Friday and said, \"It's already there in the PDF document that I shared on Friday, so we have to follow the same steps. So that's all I did.\" Srikar and Saurav both said their A2G access shows \"granted\" but nothing is actually visible yet, and Namita explained the next step is to ping Justin to complete provisioning. Colin immediately assigned her the responsibility to teach the rest of the team the access procedure.\n\nNamita also added the single most precise technical clarification of the meeting, correcting the common-misread ADS acronym:\n\n> Aurora Development Server. So it's not the Active Directory server.\n\nShe drew out from the Apr 10 call the distinction between the CI track (Basel dev builds, which she has visibility into) and the CD track (nightly builds, which Justin has not shown her). She committed to closing the CD gap this week.\n\n### Srikar as the on-site clarifier\n\nSrikar was not the loudest voice in the meeting but contributed precise corrections at key moments. Two specific instances:\n\n1. On the Scribble tool ownership: Colin was unsure whether Naga or Justin handled Scribble. Srikar confirmed, \"This is with Naga Colin.\"\n2. On the Scribble tool state: Late in the meeting, Srikar clarified that Scribble is not deployed or integrated with any system. It is a local Python script that runs Wispr on an audio file. This single statement shifted Colin's framing from \"they have a working tool\" to \"they have a POC that is nowhere near production,\" which set up the diplomatic architecture-diagram approach Colin then coached the team on.\n\n### Vaishali as quiet observer\n\nVaishali said nothing substantive during the meeting. Colin acknowledged her by name at the open and closed, and noted he had just had a one-on-one with her. She is still waiting on Cisco hardware, which means the Codex and Copilot access requests do not apply to her this week. Her work is scoped to pair with Namita on the build-log side once her hardware arrives. No sentiment signal either way can be drawn from a single-utterance meeting. Her engagement level will become readable in later sets.\n\n### Team structure formalized\n\nThe meeting explicitly divided the BayOne team into two tracks:\n\n- **WebEx scraping and transcription track:** Srikar (lead on-site) and Saurav (offshore counterpart). Colin will meet with them separately on architecture.\n- **Build logs track:** Namita (lead on-site) and Vaishali (offshore partner). Colin will meet with them separately on architecture.\n\nColin confirmed he will handle the master Srinivas meetings but said each track should start generating its own presentations using the Singularity tool going forward, so that track-level visibility does not depend on Colin personally.\n\n---\n\n## New People Introduced\n\nNo new people were introduced in this meeting.\n\nTwo notes on people already on the org chart:\n\n1. **Vaishali's first team-meeting appearance was 2026-04-13, not 2026-04-17.** The org chart currently references her onboarding as Apr 17 based on the Friday Sync. This meeting confirms she was already present and observing on Apr 13. The org chart should be updated to reflect this earlier first-team-contact date.\n2. **Tanuja Raj was not in this meeting.** She first appeared in the Friday Sync (Apr 17). No update required to her entry.\n\n---\n\n## External parties referenced but not present\n\n- **Srinivas Pitta** (Cisco Director of Engineering, AI lead): referenced throughout. The entire meeting was partly a debrief of his Apr 10 call, and several action items this week are framed around what to send him and when to escalate to him.\n- **Justin Joseph** (Cisco build infrastructure owner): referenced as the next-step provisioning contact for A2G access and as the source for CD nightly build visibility. Also referenced as someone who is on the Cisco side of future scribble-versus-WebEx meeting-transcript comparison testing.\n- **Nagabhushan (\"Naga\")** (Cisco, Pulse and Scribble owner): referenced as the actual owner of Scribble. Colin and Saurav agreed the team needs to connect with Naga directly to understand whether the WebEx-side work overlaps with what BayOne is being asked to build.\n- **Imran** (Cisco stakeholder on the Apr 10 call): referenced as the person who raised the ADS-machine subdivision concern and then went quiet when Colin brought up auto-scaling, which Colin read as Imran not having thought in those terms before.\n- **Anupma** (Cisco, CAT database / DevEx): referenced indirectly as \"An Purma\" in the Apr 10 call context. She raised the finite-ADS-compute point.\n\n---\n\n## Sentiment summary\n\nNo sentiment shifts from existing org-chart entries. Vaishali is a new data point with insufficient signal. Namita's credibility as a technically serious contributor is reinforced by her correct acronym call on ADS, her successful GitHub access, and her clear articulation of the CI versus CD gap. Saurav's reputation as the strongest offshore contributor is reinforced by the quality of his architectural thinking in this meeting.\n\n---\n\n## Files in this set (planned)\n\n- `07_standup_people_2026-04-13.md` — this file\n- `07_standup_action_items_2026-04-13.md` — parallel agent\n- `07_standup_blockers_2026-04-13.md` — parallel agent\n- `07_standup_decisions_2026-04-13.md` — parallel agent\n- `07_standup_technical_discussion_2026-04-13.md` — parallel agent\n- `07_standup_summary_2026-04-13.md` — last, main session\n- Bridge: `06g-07_changes_2026-04-13.md` — retroactive gap-fill bridge\n"}

This meeting had significant technical content: WebEx architecture, transcription A/B testing methodology, ADS infrastructure explanation, build log data modeling, and architectural critique of Cisco's current work.\n\n**Read this file:** /home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/source/internal_team_meet_4-13-2026.txt\n\n**Context from the topic map:**\n- BayOne internal Monday team-planning meeting (Apr 13, 2026)\n- Attendees: Colin Moore, Namita Ravikiran Mane, Saurav Kumar Mishra, Srikar Madarapu, Vaishali Sonawane\n- Meeting serves partly as architecture coaching session. Colin delivers three substantial tutorials during the call: Podman/Docker at Cisco, ADS machine internals, A/B testing methodology for Scribble-vs-WebEx transcription.\n\n**What to capture in exhaustive detail:**\n\n1. **WebEx developer platform taxonomy** (Saurav's detailed breakdown): the Cisco WebEx developer setup includes integrations, service apps, bots, agentic apps, and OAuth flows. Saurav's proposed architecture: a service-level app that scrapes data to a DB, an MCP over that DB, then a bot or app on top. Decoupled. OAuth-driven permissions so scraped data respects per-user access. Concern about cross-chat contamination if using bot tokens (bots have access to all spaces the bot was invited to).\n\n2. **Cisco's WebEx bot compliance policy**: Saurav hit this when deploying his bot. Nested policy docs (bot policy linked to internet policy linked to 2-3 more policies). Key constraint: bots cannot be in groups with <2 users or >100 users. Cisco's own NXOS WebEx group has ~300 users, violating this. Saurav's read: Cisco can adjust their own rule, but third-party bots must register with the org.\n\n3. **Podman vs Docker at Cisco**: Cisco pushed Podman because it's FOSS (licensing reasons for Docker). Saurav already runs Podman successfully for the Wall-E / Volley bot with a PG17 Postgres image pulled from AWS public container registry (no Docker auth needed). Commands are near-identical (podman ps = docker ps, podman compose = docker compose). Colin recommends team gets Docker Desktop too for POC flexibility, with diplomatic framing to Srinivas.\n\n4. **ADS machine internals** (Colin's tutorial): ADS = Aurora Development Server (Namita clarified). Likely running on a Proxmox-class hypervisor managing subdivided VM slices. Linux OS (likely RHEL or Debian given Cisco's Red Hat licensing), which means concurrent multi-user access works by default (unlike Windows). Built with pre-installed packages analogous to a Dockerfile. Justin confirmed to Namita that one shared ADS suffices; team can work in parallel on the same machine.\n\n5. **ADS subdivision question** (Anupma's concern on Apr 10 call, discussed here): Cisco has a finite pool of compute for ADS machines across the org. Colin's decision: do NOT subdivide per-person yet; wait until there is a tactical production-related reason. Future state should auto-scale (like Docker/Kubernetes) — spin up new ADS instances based on utilization. This is a BayOne value-add Cisco isn't thinking about yet.\n\n6. **Scribble-vs-WebEx A/B testing three-tier framework** (Colin's tutorial):\n   - Tier 1: Raw quality — out-of-box Wispr vs out-of-box Cisco WebEx transcription. What specifically does Wispr do better? (Likely jargon/names because of fine-tuning.)\n   - Tier 2: Glossary-augmented quality — can a simple prompt-engineering layer (meeting attendees list, commonly missed terms) close the gap on the cheaper option? Downstream AI models are fairly tolerant of transcript errors.\n   - Tier 3: Compute and scale cost — if Wispr is self-hosted (as Scribble currently is), it's not multi-threaded for 500 concurrent meeting transcripts. What's the per-request cost? If Wispr is $20K/month for a 10% accuracy improvement, the answer is no.\n   - Ultimate question: does the accuracy improvement matter for downstream AI use? Probably not much.\n\n7. **Build log data modeling** (Colin's framework for Namita):\n   - Use a **star schema**, NOT NoSQL/JSON. Star schema enables easier agentic-AI tool integration.\n   - Key on **SHA hashes** for commits and PRs. These are unique and queryable from Git directly.\n   - **Do not persist everything.** For Git-retrievable data (commit lineage, PR contents), store only the identifier; build an MCP tool that re-queries Git on demand.\n   - Good candidates for persistence: IDs, SHA hashes, metadata (author, timestamp, build ID, log file provenance), derived analytics.\n   - Model should support backward traceability: from a build outcome, trace back to contributing PRs and commits.\n\n8. **Log file structure unknowns** (observation-period rationale):\n   - Unknown whether logs are composite (one giant text file appended from multiple sources) or individual files per application\n   - Unknown what other log types exist beyond Bazel: Jenkins logs? DevX logs? Build logs? Unclear.\n   - Namita confirmed CI (Bazel dev builds) vs CD (nightly builds) distinction. She has CI visibility, not CD.\n   - Plan: observation period to capture logs over multiple days, map log types and sources, then architect the processing approach.\n\n9. **Naga's Scribble architecture assessment** (Srikar's clarification, Colin's framing):\n   - Scribble is a ~50-line Python script that runs Wispr on an audio file locally.\n   - Not integrated with anything. Not a service. No backend observer for WebEx meetings.\n   - Not production-ready; a POC at best.\n   - Implication: BayOne can propose a proper service-layer architecture without stepping on Naga's toes, because there's nothing real to replace.\n   - Also implies a nontrivial amount of future work (observer backend to find new WebEx meetings, pull audio, pipe to Wispr, store transcript).\n\n10. **Cisco's \"solution without a use case\" pattern** (Colin's critique):\n    - The Scribble project and the broader Pulse/Scribble WebEx tooling appear to be built without a clear business use case.\n    - Colin's read: \"someone messed around at work and got paid to do it.\"\n    - What Cisco needs to hear: \"here's how it works today, here's how we suggest it works in the future.\" Architecture diagrams do the convincing; don't explicitly call the current work bad.\n    - Engineering culture note: \"70% of what they tell you verbally will be correct; 30% is misremembered, misstated, or misrepresented per the database, not intentionally.\"\n\n11. **Presentation generation via Singularity**: Colin did the Apr 10 presentation in 15 minutes using Singularity. Plans to train each team so presentation generation becomes a team-level responsibility. Removes bottleneck on Colin.\n\n12. **Covert Claude Code use**: Colin has Claude Code installed on his Cisco machine. Team should use it but not mention it in meetings with Srinivas. Codex and Copilot access requests are in progress as the overt alternative.\n\n**IMPORTANT: Speech-to-text transcript quality.**\nSame correction dictionary as other agents:\n- \"Bay One\" / \"P1\" / \"B1\" = \"BayOne\"\n- \"Basel\" = \"Bazel\"\n- \"Gmake\" / \"GMIC\" = \"Gmake\"\n- \"codecs\" = \"Codex\"\n- \"Co-work\" / \"cloud code\" = \"Claude Code\"\n- \"Wispr\" = \"Whisper\"\n- \"Deepak Site\" = \"DeepSight\"\n- \"Scribble\" / \"Scribbler\" = \"Scribbler\"\n- \"Pulse\" = WebEx scraping tool\n- \"ADS\" = \"Aurora Development Server\"\n- \"A2G\" = GitHub Enterprise access group\n- \"NFS\" = Network File System\n- \"Srinivas\" / \"Srini\" = \"Srinivas Pitta\"\n- \"Mane\" in \"logs on Mane\" = \"main\" (Git branch)\n- \"REL\" = \"RHEL\" (Red Hat Enterprise Linux)\nInfer from context.\n\n**Write your output to:** /home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/research/07_standup_technical_discussion_2026-04-13.md\n\n**Use this header format:**\n```markdown\n# 07 - Standup: Technical Discussion\n\n**Source:** /home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/source/internal_team_meet_4-13-2026.txt\n**Source Date:** 2026-04-13 (Monday team plan for the week)\n**Document Set:** 07 (Internal BayOne team planning meeting following the April 10 Srinivas call)\n**Pass:** Focused deep dive on technical content\n\n---\n```\n\nStructure with the 12 numbered sections above, each with a clear header. This is a material shift from prior assumed framing. It reframes the strategic opportunity: there is essentially nothing in production to replace, which means BayOne's proposal work can position current-state and proposed-state architecture without political friction.\n\n### Namita as the de-facto onboarding resource\n\nNamita is the only team member who successfully completed GitHub Enterprise access using the PDF she shared on Friday. Colin assigned her to teach the rest of the team the procedure. She also corrected the ADS acronym (Aurora Development Server, not Active Directory) and introduced the CI-versus-CD distinction, which created the observation-period workstream.\n\n### Cisco IT flagged Saurav's Wall-E bot\n\nSaurav received an email from Cisco IT stating his deployed bot is non-compliant: it must be registered with the organization, and bots are restricted to groups between 2 and 100 users. Cisco's own NXOS group has approximately 300 users, which violates the bot's own policy. This adds a compliance workstream that did not exist in prior sets.\n\n## Status of the main workstreams\n\n**WebEx scraping and transcription (Srikar and Saurav):**\n- A/B testing framework defined: three tiers (raw quality, glossary-augmented quality, compute and scale cost).\n- Next dependencies: access to Scribbler repo (blocked by DeepSight access), verbal understanding from Naga about existing WebEx work, and alignment on whether Cisco's current Pulse and Scribbler work overlaps with BayOne scope.\n- Compliance blocker to resolve: bot registration and group-size policy conflict.\n- Saurav proposed a decoupled architecture (scraper plus DB plus MCP plus app or bot) that Colin explicitly endorsed as the right direction.\n\n**Build log analysis (Namita and Vaishali):**\n- Bazel confirmed as the only relevant log type (Gmake ruled out by Srinivas on Apr 10).\n- Namita has GitHub and temporary ADS access, has visibility into CI (Bazel dev builds), but lacks CD (nightly builds) visibility.\n- Plan: observation period of past and future logs on the temporary ADS machine; structured storage by date, build, source, build type; automation script rather than manual downloads.\n- Data model direction: star schema with SHA-hash keys, MCP tool for on-the-fly Git queries, persist only what cannot be regenerated from Git.\n- Blocker: traceability gap (Justin told Colin that build-to-commit lineage is not currently captured, which Colin finds surprising since it is metadata).\n\n**Cross-cutting infrastructure and tooling:**\n- GitHub Enterprise access in various states across the team (Namita has it working, Srikar and Saurav show \"granted\" but no visible repos and need to ping Justin, Vaishali is gated on hardware).\n- Codex and Copilot access requests submitted via appstore.cisco.com this week for everyone except Vaishali.\n- Podman usable today; Docker Desktop request pending IT approval; Saurav has a working Podman-based Wall-E bot using a PG17 Postgres image from AWS's public container registry.\n- Claude Code remains in covert use. Colin has it personally installed. Team is not to mention it in meetings with Srinivas. Codex and Copilot are the overt alternative once access is granted.\n\n## Files in this set\n\n- `07_standup_people_2026-04-13.md` — attendance, roles, dynamics, new people\n- `07_standup_action_items_2026-04-13.md` — 35 new items plus carry-forward status on prior items\n- `07_standup_blockers_2026-04-13.md` — access, compliance, knowledge, scope, hardware, tool-usage, political, and cadence blockers\n- `07_standup_decisions_2026-04-13.md` — 17 decisions plus deferred items and cross-cutting themes\n- `07_standup_technical_discussion_2026-04-13.md` — 12 sections covering WebEx architecture, transcription A/B testing, ADS internals, build log data modeling, and architectural critique of Cisco's current work\n- `07_standup_summary_2026-04-13.md` — this file\n\n## Bridge document\n\nA separate bridge document at `06g-07_changes_2026-04-13.md` captures what this set validates, invalidates, or adds relative to the previously processed Sets 01 through 06g. This bridge is retroactive: Set 07's source date (Apr 13) predates most of the other team sets (which span Apr 10 through Apr 21), so the bridge frames Set 07 as a gap fill in the chronological record rather than the next step forward in time.\n\n## What is next\n\n- Colin to assign GitHub issues for A/B testing workstreams and log observation script\n- Colin to train each team on Singularity presentation generation this week\n- Separate architecture meetings for each track (WebEx and logs)\n- Colin to escalate access blockers to Srinivas on Wednesday if still outstanding\n- Team to connect with Naga and Justin to understand existing WebEx and Scribbler state and to unblock A2G provisioning\n- Namita to teach the rest of the team GitHub Enterprise access using her Friday PDF\n- Update parent org chart to reflect Vaishali's earlier first-team-contact date (Apr 13, not Apr 17)\n"}

## User

Tool Result: 1	# Bridge Document: 01 -> 02 (Call Prep to Meeting)
2	
3	**Set 01:** Call Prep (pre-meeting assumptions and strategy)
4	**Set 02:** Meeting Transcript (what actually happened on 2026-03-12)
5	
6	---
7	
8	This document captures what changed between BayOne's pre-call preparation and what was actually said and learned during the discovery call. It tracks which hypotheses were validated, invalidated, or remain open.
9	
10	---
11	
12	## Hypotheses: Validated, Invalidated, or Open
13	
14	### Hypothesis 1: "Failing guardrails" likely means Purview DLP, not Sentinel
15	**Status: UNRESOLVED / REFRAMED**
16	
17	The call prep assumed Lam might be confusing Sentinel (SIEM) with Purview (DLP). In the actual meeting, neither Sentinel nor Purview was mentioned by the Lam team. The problem was framed entirely differently: they described trying ML models (Transformers, SpaCy, Azure AI model) for detection/redaction and getting 20% false positive rates. When Colin asked about Azure AI Foundry and Purview, Mikhail said "I don't know what that even means." The "failing guardrails" narrative from the prep turned out to be the wrong frame entirely -- this is about ML model accuracy on a custom NER/classification problem, not about misconfigured Microsoft tools.
18	
19	### Hypothesis 2: Microsoft's OOTB Sensitive Information Types don't cover semiconductor IP
20	**Status: VALIDATED (implicitly)**
21	
22	The Lam team never mentioned SITs, Purview, or any Microsoft content detection tools. Their entire effort was focused on training their own models. The fact that they went straight to custom ML (not Microsoft's built-in detection) confirms that OOTB patterns were never considered adequate for their domain-specific IP (customer names, fab identifiers, process parameters).
23	
24	### Hypothesis 3: Custom SIT configuration is non-trivial and likely not fully implemented
25	**Status: SUPERSEDED**
26	
27	This hypothesis assumed Lam was trying to use Microsoft's tooling and struggling. In reality, they bypassed Microsoft's content detection entirely and built their own ML pipeline. The hypothesis was asking the wrong question -- it's not about SIT configuration, it's about custom model training for domain-specific entities.
28	
29	### Hypothesis 4: Shadow AI may be a bigger factor than admitted
30	**Status: OPEN / NOT DISCUSSED**
31	
32	Shadow AI was not meaningfully discussed in the meeting. Brad and Mikhail focused entirely on their two use cases (search redaction, entry-point detection). The shadow AI concern from the call prep -- that banning all AI drives usage underground -- was never raised or explored. This remains an open question for future conversation, but it was explicitly not part of what Lam wanted to discuss.
33	
34	---
35	
36	## What We Got Wrong (or Didn't Anticipate)
37	
38	### 1. The problem framing was different than expected
39	The call prep framed this as a "guardrails failing" problem -- implying misconfigured enterprise tools. The meeting revealed it's a **custom NER/classification problem**: how to detect customer-identifiable information in unstructured text with a false positive rate below 1%, when all ML approaches tried so far produce ~20%.
40	
41	### 2. Two distinct use cases, not one
42	The call prep treated the problem as a single "detection and redaction" challenge. Mikhail explicitly separated it into two business cases with different requirements:
43	- **Self-help search** (batch redaction, accuracy-focused, over-redacting OK)
44	- **Escalation entry** (real-time detection, performance-sensitive, false positives unacceptable)
45	
46	These are "separate swim lanes" per Mikhail.
47	
48	### 3. They've tried more than expected
49	The prep suggested Lam might not have fully implemented custom detection. In reality, they've tried three ML models, set up MLOps on Azure, attempted rule-based approaches, and evaluated a 1,000+ hour labeling exercise. They are further along than the prep assumed -- they've hit real technical walls, not configuration gaps.
50	
51	### 4. Generative AI was deliberately avoided
52	The prep didn't anticipate that Lam would have a philosophical objection to LLMs for this use case. Mikhail explicitly stated they chose ML over generative AI because they want deterministic "redact/don't redact" binary output, not unstructured LLM output. This shapes what BayOne can propose.
53	
54	### 5. The infrastructure is far more fragmented than assumed
55	The prep mentioned "Azure stack: AI Foundry + Microsoft Sentinel." The reality is dramatically more complex: on-prem, cloud, LamGPT, Copilot, cloud bots, 6+ search systems, no unified data lake, and "anything you can think of" per Mikhail. The call prep was far too optimistic about infrastructure coherence.
56	
57	### 6. Brad's "we've heard it before" posture
58	The prep suggested Brad was a structured, organized contact. The meeting revealed he is also deeply skeptical of vendors who jump to solutions. He explicitly set a gate: BayOne must demonstrate problem understanding ("repeat back") before any solution discussion is permitted. This was the defining dynamic of the meeting and was not anticipated in the prep.
59	
60	---
61	
62	## What We Got Right
63	
64	### 1. IP protection is existential
65	The call prep correctly identified that cross-customer data contamination is catastrophic for Lam. The meeting confirmed this in detail: customer segmentation is enforced at the ticket level, employees are physically identified by colored badges based on trade restriction status, and violations are found immediately when you look (7th ticket).
66	
67	### 2. Lam is security-sophisticated
68	The CISO quote in the prep about zero-trust was validated by the meeting's operational details: IAM program, TRI flags, embargo country restrictions, ASM for sensitive areas, and deliberate over-restriction as the default philosophy.
69	
70	### 3. The spelling variation problem is real
71	The prep mentioned "Fab11, F11, fab-11, FAP space 11" as examples. The meeting confirmed this exact problem and added "Micro 11" to the list. This remains the core technical challenge for any detection approach.
72	
73	### 4. Colin's experience is directly relevant
74	The Coherent Corp experience, ITAR/CMMC background, and AI governance at scale all resonated. Colin's best moment (the 20%/ChatGPT diagnosis) directly leveraged this experience.
75	
76	### 5. Brad is the decision maker
77	The prep identified Brad as the gatekeeper. The meeting confirmed he owns the full vertical: business case, product definition, ideation, and execution.
78	
79	---
80

That framing does not apply because Set 07's events happened eight days before Set 06g's events. Instead, this bridge documents:\n\n1. What Set 07 validates, contradicts, or adds relative to what prior sets had inferred about the April 13 time period\n2. What threads in Set 07 were subsequently developed in Sets 02 through 06g\n3. How Set 07 reshapes the reader's understanding of the blockchain narrative when read in chronological order\n\n---\n\n## What Set 07 Confirms From Prior Sets\n\n### Confirm: Namita as the engagement's most methodical team member\n\nSets 01 through 06 established Namita as a detail-oriented, methodical contributor who had begun surfacing architecture thinking by Set 02. Set 07 confirms this profile one meeting earlier than previously recorded. On April 13 she had already successfully completed GitHub Enterprise access (the only team member to do so), had produced a PDF with the exact steps, and corrected Colin on the ADS acronym (Aurora Development Server, not Active Directory). Her CI-versus-CD distinction in this meeting is the same thread that developed into the detailed build log architecture work captured in Sets 02a, 02c, and 04e.\n\n### Confirm: Saurav as the strongest offshore technical contributor\n\nSets 01, 02, and 04f positioned Saurav as the offshore team member who did the most substantive architecture thinking. Set 07 shows the same pattern on April 13. His decoupled architecture proposal (scraper plus database plus MCP plus app or bot) in this meeting is almost verbatim what Set 04f captures three days later as his proposed WebEx architecture. The ideas did not originate at the Apr 16 sync, they originated at or before this Apr 13 meeting.\n\n### Confirm: Srikar as the on-site clarifier, not the originator\n\nSets 03_sync and 03_sync_rui_discovery showed Srikar as the person who brought back key facts from Cisco but rarely originated architecture. Set 07 confirms this. His most substantive contributions in this meeting were factual corrections on Scribbler ownership (Naga, not Justin) and Scribbler state (local script, not integrated). Both of those facts reshape the meeting's framing but neither is an original idea of his.\n\n### Confirm: Colin's architecture-diplomacy framing\n\nSet 05_prep_pulse_scribble_naming_and_naga_stance captures Colin's strategy for diplomatically addressing the Scribbler scope overlap with Naga. Set 07 shows the same framing in earlier form: the architecture-diagram-first approach, the \"let smart people come to their own conclusions\" rule, and the \"you can't get your feathers ruffled if it's not really working for real yet\" principle were already fully formed at the Apr 13 meeting.\n\n---\n\n## What Set 07 Adds That Was Not Previously Recorded\n\n### New: Vaishali's first team-meeting appearance is Apr 13, not Apr 17\n\nThe existing parent org chart marks Vaishali as onboarded in the Apr 17 Friday Sync (processed as Set 05 prep materials). Set 07 documents her attendance at the Apr 13 team meeting four days earlier. She was silent except for closing acknowledgment, but she was present and Colin opened the meeting noting he had just come from a one-on-one with her. Her first-team-contact date needs to move from Apr 17 to Apr 13 in the parent org chart.\n\n### New: Two-track team structure was formalized on Apr 13, not later\n\nSets 02 through 06g reference the two-track structure (WebEx and build logs) as if it were an assumed operating model. Set 07 is the meeting where that structure was explicitly defined and communicated. Colin announced the split during this meeting and told each track they would have separate architecture meetings with him. This is the origin of the structure, not a mid-engagement reorganization.\n\n### New: Wednesday escalation cadence\n\nPrior sets had no explicit escalation schedule. Set 07 establishes Wednesday as the latest day of the week to flag access blockers to Srinivas. The rationale is Srinivas's explicit Apr 10 ask to hear about blockers earlier in the week so he can help.\n\n### New: Weekly presentation cadence as team-owned work\n\nSets 05_prep and 05a document the Apr 17 presentation preparation with Colin clearly owning the slide work. Set 07 captures the plan to move presentation generation from Colin-owned to team-owned. Each track will generate architecture diagrams and Singularity-produced presentations weekly. Training was planned for the Apr 13 afternoon and subsequent days.\n\n### New: Cisco IT flag on Saurav's Wall-E bot\n\nSet 07 documents an email Saurav received from Cisco IT flagging his deployed Wall-E bot as non-compliant: the bot must be registered with the organization, and bots are restricted to groups between 2 and 100 users. Cisco's own NXOS WebEx group is approximately 300 users, which the bot's own policy violates. This compliance blocker does not appear in prior sets. It is not clear whether the team ever returned to this item; the subsequent Namita incident on Apr 20 reshaped the compliance landscape in a different direction (data handling violations, not bot registration).\n\n### New: Scribbler's actual architectural state\n\nSets 02d (Srikar's WebEx recording extraction), 04f (Saurav's WebEx architecture), and 05_prep_pulse_scribble_naming_and_naga_stance all discuss Scribbler as if its internal architecture were partially known. Set 07 captures the specific moment Srikar clarified that Scribbler is a ~50-line Python script running Whisper locally on a selected audio file. It is not integrated with any Cisco system. Colin shifted his entire framing based on this clarification: from \"they have a working tool we need to work around\" to \"they have a POC that BayOne's proposed architecture can replace without political friction.\"\n\n### New: A/B testing three-tier methodology\n\nSet 07 is the origin of the Scribbler-versus-WebEx A/B testing framework that later sets reference. The three tiers are (1) raw quality, (2) glossary-augmented quality, and (3) compute and scale cost. Set 07 is where this framework was articulated by Colin.\n\n### New: Build log data model principles\n\nSet 07 captures the star-schema call, the SHA-hash-as-key principle, the MCP-over-Git query approach, and the \"persist only what cannot be regenerated\" rule. Subsequent sets (02c, 04e) develop these further but Set 07 is the origin.\n\n---\n\n## What Set 07 Contradicts or Refines\n\n### Refinement: ADS machine model\n\nPrior sets reference ADS machines as infrastructure BayOne is trying to access. Set 07 adds the specific model: ADS is Aurora Development Server, Linux-based (likely RHEL or Debian), supports concurrent multi-user access (unlike a Windows VM), and Justin confirmed one shared ADS is sufficient for the team rather than one per person. Prior sets did not articulate this.\n\n### Refinement: Nature of the Codex versus Claude Code tension\n\nSet 02 and Set 04 touch on Codex being Srinivas's preferred tool. Set 07 adds a specific instruction from Colin: Claude Code is in covert use, installed on his Cisco machine personally, and must not be mentioned to Srinivas. Codex and Copilot are the overt alternative once access is granted. This is more operationally specific than prior sets.\n\n### Refinement: Team split origin and timing\n\nPrior sets imply the WebEx versus logs split existed from Apr 10 or earlier. Set 07 shows the split was formalized on Apr 13, three days after the Srinivas meeting. The split was clearly latent in the Apr 10 meeting but was operationalized here.\n\n---\n\n## Threads Opened in Set 07 That Later Sets Developed\n\nThe following threads appear first in Set 07 and are developed in subsequent sets:\n\n| Thread | Introduced in Set 07 | Developed in |\n|--------|---------------------|--------------|\n| Decoupled WebEx architecture (scraper, DB, MCP, app or bot) | Saurav's proposal | Set 04f (Saurav architecture) |\n| Three-tier A/B testing for transcription | Colin's framework | Set 04d (Srikar NxOS chat analysis mentions comparison) |\n| Star schema data model for build logs | Colin's direction to Namita | Sets 02a, 02c, 04e (Namita build log architecture) |\n| Team ownership of presentation generation | Colin's training plan | Set 05a (Srikar's updated pain point charts) |\n| Wednesday escalation cadence | Colin's rule | Set 03_sync (blockers and access status discussion) |\n| Build log observation period | Colin's plan | Set 02a (Namita build log analysis updates) |\n| Architecture-diagram-first diplomacy re Scribbler | Colin's framework | Set 05_prep_pulse_scribble_naming_and_naga_stance |\n\n## Threads That Did Not Continue\n\nA small number of threads opened in Set 07 do not appear to have been developed in later sets, or were superseded:\n\n- **Bot compliance workstream (Cisco IT flag on Wall-E)**: no explicit follow-up in Sets 02 through 06g. Saurav continued building bots; compliance implications were not revisited in writing. The Apr 20 Namita incident reshaped the compliance conversation toward data handling rather than bot policy, which may have absorbed attention.\n- **Request for last Friday call transcript from Scribbler**: Saurav proposed at the end of the meeting that the team ask Justin for the Apr 10 call's Scribbler transcript as baseline test data. No subsequent set records whether this was requested or provided.\n- **Podman-based Wall-E bot as working reference**: Saurav's Podman plus Postgres PG17 setup (from AWS public container registry) was presented as a replicable pattern for the team. No subsequent set documents the team adopting or extending this pattern.\n\n---\n\n## Net Effect of Adding Set 07 to the Chain\n\nReading the team chain in source-date order (which requires mentally inserting Set 07 before Set 01), Set 07 is the origin point for most of the structural and architectural frameworks that later sets reference. It is reasonable to treat Set 07 as the de facto methodology set for the team's operating model during the Apr 10 to Apr 21 window, alongside the dedicated methodology file.\n\nFor future chain readers: if chronological reconstruction matters (e.g., for a proposal writing session or a contract renewal briefing), read Set 07 immediately after the Main chain's Set 11 (Apr 10 Srinivas meeting) and before the team chain's Set 01 (Apr 10 standup). Otherwise, the processing order is fine for operational use.\n"}

7	
8	---
9	
10	## Introduction
11	
12	This set captures the action items and assignments generated during the BayOne internal Monday team planning call on April 13, 2026. The meeting followed the April 10 call with Srinivas Pitta and was used to recap what came out of that conversation, align on the week's direction, and assign concrete next steps across the two parallel tracks (WebEx scraping and transcription with Srikar and Saurav, build logs with Namita and Vaishali). Many items carry forward from prior sets (especially access-related blockers already tracked as items 1-38), while several new items are introduced, including GitHub account creation for BayOne-side repository management, A/B testing of Scribbler against the built-in WebEx transcription, a data model exercise for build log traceability, and a new standing weekly deliverable cadence for architecture diagrams and team presentations.
13	
14	---
15	
16	## New Action Items (from Set 07)
17	
18	| # | Action | Owner | Track | Urgency | Source context |
19	|---|--------|-------|-------|---------|----------------|
20	| 39 | Create BayOne GitHub account so Colin can add her to the team and assign tracked project-level to-do items | Namita | Cross-cutting | Today (during meeting) | Srikar already created his BayOne GitHub account. Colin cannot assign project-level tasks on GitHub until all team members have accounts. Namita agreed to do it immediately. |
21	| 40 | Request access to Codex and GitHub Copilot via appstore.cisco.com on Cisco hardware | Namita, Srikar, Saurav (everyone with Cisco hardware) | Cross-cutting | Today | Vaishali is excluded because she is still waiting on hardware. Colin framed this as a basic per-person access request. |
22	| 41 | Request Podman access and install Docker Desktop (Docker Desktop requires an access request) via appstore.cisco.com | All (Cisco hardware holders) | Cross-cutting | This week | Saurav flagged that Podman is already provisioned because Cisco DeepSight and ADS deployments use it, while Docker Desktop requires an access request. Colin wants Docker locally for POC work so the team can spin up anything under the sun and avoid being blocked by database access. |
23	| 42 | Assign a rotating person from each of the two tracks (WebEx, Logs) to generate Singularity-style pre-meeting slide decks for the Srinivas calls | Colin to assign, one person per track | Cross-cutting | This week (training first) | Colin will handle the master meeting deck himself but wants trained backups on each team for continuity. Slide content should separate topics cleanly (for example, not blend WebEx scraping and logs into the same slide). |
24	| 43 | Before showing any proposed architecture to Srinivas, first get access to whatever Naga and Justin have already built for WebEx scraping and transcription, inspect the repo, and document what exists | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week (gated on repo access from Srinivas/Naga) | Colin: "Srinivas said that he would unblock that, so I'm going to keep on, you know, bugging him until that happens." Access needed to ADS, DeepSight, and the GitHub repositories. Colin warned that whatever Naga and Justin say verbally will be about seventy percent correct and thirty percent misremembered or misstated, so verify in the code. |
25	| 44 | If Naga and Justin demonstrate what they have built in a call, record the meeting and then build an architecture diagram of what exists as-is | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | As soon as a demo call is scheduled | Colin directed that even if what they have built looks bad, still draw the as-is architecture and then propose a different way alongside it. |
26	| 45 | Execute A/B testing comparing Scribbler (Naga's Whisper-based tool) against the built-in WebEx meeting transcription | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week (gated on DeepSight / Scribbler access) | Naga told Srikar on the April 10 call that the team can build on Scribbler in parallel without depending on WebEx. Colin wants the A/B framed across three tiers: (1) raw out-of-the-box quality, (2) simple glossary or prompt-engineering uplift to the WebEx transcript (for example, feeding the downstream AI a speech-to-text caveat with a list of commonly missed terms and meeting attendee names), (3) production considerations including compute, concurrency, cost, and how many transcripts can be processed in parallel. Colin cautioned that Whisper running locally is typically not multi-threaded. |
27	| 46 | Measure and report the downstream-AI usability of each transcript, not just raw transcription accuracy | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week | Colin's framing: even if Whisper is ten percent more accurate, does the downstream AI summarization or extraction step actually care? If AI produces the same output from either transcript, then Whisper's accuracy premium does not justify the compute cost. |
28	| 47 | Capture and report compute, resource, and concurrency cost figures for Whisper at scale as part of the A/B write-up | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week | Colin: if Whisper is ten percent better but costs twenty thousand dollars a month versus free for the built-in, no VP will approve that. VPs are rewarded at year-end for coming in under budget. |
29	| 48 | Connect with Naga to clarify how the team is supposed to test Scribbler (since Saurav confirmed no API keys appear to be provisioned) | Srikar | WebEx | This week | Saurav pointed out that Scribbler appears to be a local Python script running Whisper, roughly fifty lines of code, with no deployment, no integrated observer backend, and no provisioned keys. The team cannot actually test Scribbler in its current state without Naga's help. |
30	| 49 | Ask Naga or Justin for the Scribbler-produced transcript of the April 10 Friday call (since Justin was on that call) and compare it to the WebEx transcript for the same meeting | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week (quick win before repo access lands) | Saurav's proposal: while repo access is still pending, use the April 10 call as the first concrete A/B data point. Colin agreed this is "probably a message away from him." |
31	| 50 | Determine whether Scribbler is automatically triggered when Justin joins a WebEx call or whether Justin runs it manually each time | Srikar, Saurav | WebEx | This week | Colin wants this documented because the answer determines whether any real observer or pipeline backend exists. Srikar clarified on the call that Scribbler is not configured to any existing systems and must be run manually on any local audio file. |
32	| 51 | For the WebEx scraper side, design the architecture as a decoupled service layer (scraper, database, MCP, then downstream apps or bots on top) rather than a single monolithic bot, and include OAuth so the bot accesses data using the user's permissions rather than a bot token that sees every chat and space | Saurav (lead), Srikar | WebEx | This week (feeds into Friday architecture deliverable) | Saurav proposed this pattern; Colin endorsed it, noting that the Cisco side does not appear to have thought through this and that this is where BayOne adds value. |

57	
58	## Carry-forward and Status Updates on Prior Items
59	
60	Several prior items (already tracked in the numbered action-items tracker) were explicitly addressed during this standup. Status updates from the transcript are captured below so the tracker can be reconciled.
61	
62	**Item 1 (Verify ADS machine access and inspect build logs on NFS, Namita):** Namita confirmed she has access to the temporary ADS machine and can see some logs there. However, those are past (passing) logs from the dev environment, not failure logs, because nothing has failed in the past four days in dev. Partial progress. The NFS access for main-branch real-run logs is still outstanding (now restated as item 60 in this set).
63	
64	**Item 2 (Verify GitHub repo access, review Justin's PR, Namita):** Namita confirmed that GitHub access works. She can see Justin's Python script and the PR he raised. She accomplished this by following the steps in the PDF document she shared with the team on Friday. Complete for Namita. Srikar and Saurav both have A2G group access granted but cannot yet see repositories; the remaining step is for Namita to walk the team through her PDF steps and then to ping Justin for additional provisioning (captured as items 66 and 67 in this set).
65	
66	**Item 4 (Raise Divakar conflict with Srinivas):** Resolved on the April 10 call; Srinivas decided the team should focus on Bazel only and ignore Gmake. This also retires the Bazel-vs-Gmake question as an open item (see discussion below).
67	
68	**Item 5 (Clarify WebEx scraper scope with Srinivas):** Partially answered on the April 10 call. Srinivas and Naga indicated the team can build on Scribbler in parallel without depending on WebEx, and confirmed the overall A/B-testing direction. However, Colin observed that the Cisco side is not fully decided on the end use case ("there is no actual plan, they are kind of just trying to see what works and what does not"). Scope clarification effectively continues, now as concrete A/B-testing and decoupled service-layer design work (items 45 through 53).
69	
70	**Item 6 (Get access to Pulse and Scribbler repos on DeepSight GitHub, Srikar):** Still blocked. Colin will continue to bug Srinivas. The April 10 call produced a verbal commitment from Srinivas to unblock this. Access is needed not only to the GitHub repositories but also to ADS and DeepSight. Re-articulated this week with urgency tied to the A/B testing and architecture deliverable (items 43 and 49).
71	
72	**Bazel vs Gmake decision (previously open):** Fully resolved on the April 10 call. Srinivas confirmed that the team should focus exclusively on Bazel. Gmake is explicitly out of scope. Namita double-checked this during the April 13 standup ("you are saying that we should look at Gmake logs as well right now, just for our understanding?"); Colin clarified Gmake remains excluded. However, CI versus CD is now a live distinction: CI (Bazel, dev environment) is the primary focus, while CD (nightly builds) also falls in scope because Srinivas mentioned both on the April 10 call.
73	
74	**Items 28 through 31 (Saurav's hardware and ticket escalation, Vaishali hardware):** Not discussed in detail on this call. Vaishali is still waiting on hardware and is therefore excluded from the Codex, Copilot, Podman, and Docker Desktop access-request items. Vaishali's participation in the log track is async at this stage (Namita processes, Vaishali helps process and transfer downstream).
75	
76	**Items 32 through 38 (prior escalation, Naga meetup, Nexus T, NxOS chat scraper, parquet conversion):** Not directly referenced in this standup. The new items in Set 07 effectively supersede the immediate WebEx track focus by pivoting to A/B testing of Scribbler versus built-in WebEx transcription and to a decoupled service-layer architecture.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## Standing Weekly Deliverables Introduced This Week
81	
82	Colin introduced two recurring written deliverables that each track will produce every week, starting this week. These can be done on BayOne hardware because they are documents.
83	
84	**1. Architecture diagram.** Colin will pair with each track this week to teach the team how to produce architecture diagrams in the right style ("the right kind of architecture, so I will show you how to do this that is going to make their IT team feel butterflies in their stomach and heart emojis for the architecture"). Each architecture deliverable should include a Considerations page listing areas the Cisco side has not yet thought through, so that Srinivas can make the call on whether to spend time addressing them. This week's architecture deliverables are one per track, captured as items 62 (logs) and 63 (WebEx).
85	
86	**2. Pre-meeting presentation deck.** Colin will run Singularity training this week so each team can generate its own Srinivas-call deck going forward. Each deck should open with a game-plan slide showing the high-level plan of action for the meeting, then cleanly separate topics (for example, WebEx and logs should not blend into the same slide). After training, deck generation becomes a team-level responsibility, with two people on each team trained for continuity (so vacations or illness do not break the cadence). This is captured as item 42.
87	
88	Colin also emphasized the speed of the Singularity-assisted approach: "I was able to get all of these slides done with diagrams just based upon what we talked about. Fifteen minutes prior to the call." He reassured the team: "No one is going to be wasting hours in PowerPoint here. We do not do that."
89

# 07a - Cross Reference: CI/CD-Relevant Excerpts From EPNM One-on-One

**Source:** /home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/source/week_2026-04-14/day_2026-04-14/singularity-and-cisco-deepagents-and-ui-modernizat_01.txt
**Source Date:** 2026-04-14 (60-minute Colin-Saurav one-on-one, primarily about the separate EPNM-to-EMS UI Modernization engagement)
**Document Set:** 07a (supplementary to Set 07, the Apr 13 Monday team planning meeting)
**Pass:** Targeted extraction of CI/CD-relevant content only. The bulk of this meeting concerns the EPNM engagement and is intentionally not captured here.

---

## 1. Saurav staffed on both CI/CD and EPNM simultaneously

This matters because it changes the CI/CD team roster. Saurav is now a dual-project resource, and this was decided by Colin in this meeting, not at the Apr 13 team meeting.

Colin's rationale is that CI/CD does not currently fill Saurav's bandwidth. At 01:13:30 Colin said, "you can already tell the pace of work for CICD isn't quite there. You know, I told Neha and Zahra yesterday, I was like, there's no reason why we need four people right now. Like, honestly, like, there's a lot to do, but the pace at which it's able to get done." Saurav replied, "It's like hiring them for like sitting and waiting for access."

At 01:13:51 Colin confirmed the dual assignment: "even the people that are already on the project, I can, you're already onboarded onto Cisco. You already have, you know, accounts and hardware and all that. So I can pull you into this project, which makes that margin even better." At 01:36:36 Colin restated the conclusion: "with CICD, I can kind of put you on for both because I already know CICD is at the current pace is not going to take 100% of your bandwidth. Plus, you have all the access."

Saurav also asked the CI/CD-specific access question at 01:21:16: "so do we get like access to the what do you call their whole CICD pipeline?" Colin responded at 01:21:26, "Correct. Full, full open seasoning." Saurav immediately followed with, "So we can like keep on exploring for like CICD as well while working on this," and Colin agreed.

**Implication for CI/CD engagement:** Saurav has full pipeline access and is effectively a half-time resource available to the CI/CD engagement while working EPNM in parallel. This should be reflected in the CI/CD team roster and tracking; the prior assumption of four dedicated CI/CD team members is no longer complete.

---

## 2. Srikar and Namita protected as CI/CD-only

This matters because it establishes an explicit staffing rule for the CI/CD team that is invisible in any other artifact.

At 01:33:42 Colin said he plans to check in on Srikar and Namita, then at 01:33:54 stated the protection rule directly: "like Srikar and Namita, like Srinivas, like they sit next to him. So if they disappear to go work on EPNM, he's going to get ******." Saurav acknowledged. At 01:34:05 Colin confirmed he is willing to take the risk himself ("I'm okay") but not with Srikar and Namita.

The reasoning is physical co-location and client perception. Srikar and Namita are the visible BayOne presence sitting next to Srinivas Pitta. Pulling them onto EPNM would be noticed immediately and would damage the CI/CD engagement relationship.

{"file_path":"/home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/research/08_1on1_people_2026-04-15.md","content":"# 08 - One-on-One: People and Dynamics\n\n**Source:** /home/cmoore/programming/ai_opportunities/cisco/cicd/team/source/week_2026-04-14/day_2026-04-15/colin-saurav-1on1_2026-04-15_01.txt\n**Source Date:** 2026-04-15 (Wednesday morning PDT, 38-minute Colin-Saurav one-on-one)\n**Document Set:** 08 (Colin-Saurav laptop blocker and Srinivas architecture coaching)\n**Pass:** People file, always first. Captures attendees, dynamics, context.\n\n---\n\n## Attendance\n\n| Person | Role | Engagement | Notes |\n|--------|------|------------|-------|\n| Colin Moore | Director of AI, BayOne (project lead) | Full, but time-constrained | Opened the meeting with a direct constraint: it is US tax day and he needs to leave for his accountant appointment. Plans to miss most of the afternoon team session. Compressed a full laptop-escalation plan and an architecture-strategy coaching into 38 minutes. |\n| Saurav Kumar Mishra | AI/ML Engineer, BayOne (offshore India, WebEx scraper lead) | Full | Brought the Cisco laptop hardware saga to the call. Demonstrated architectural thinking once the conversation pivoted from the laptop issue to the Srinivas deliverable. Self-directed enough to propose the service-app plus MCP plus bot refactor without prompting. |\n\nNo one else on the call. This was a pure one-on-one.\n\n## Dynamics Observed\n\n### Colin in escalation mode\n\nColin took the laptop situation personally as a procurement and partner-relationship failure, not just an operational inconvenience. He said literally, \"this is like the third time in a row that IT has been stupid\" and \"that is a problem, because I am going to tell ask for any of us what he wants to do.\" He offered unprompted to buy Saurav a replacement Mac under the BayOne SAL (Standard Approval Limit) and make Cisco pay for it, or at minimum, hold Cisco IT's two-month repair estimate up to Srinivas and Anand as evidence of a systemic problem. His framing was explicitly about protecting the team and about engagement leverage ahead of the renewal: \"we have that renewal coming up and I want him to be aware, you know, hey, these are silly things that have nothing to do with us.\"\n\nThe meeting revealed Colin's coaching style on how to write escalation content. He was specific: no color commentary, fact-driven, include the two-month repair estimate in bold, document what work was lost, and phrase the impact as \"I will have to redo that work if it is not returned.\" He explicitly told Saurav, \"do not be yourself here\" when Saurav's instinct was to downplay the impact (\"I already was working on on my Bay One machine, so I did not really lose anything\"). This is the third instance in the engagement of Colin coaching Saurav to be more assertive in written escalations.\n\n### Saurav's instinct to protect others\n\nSaurav repeatedly hesitated when Colin suggested naming individual Cisco IT support representatives in the escalation email. He said, \"I do not want to get those support guys in a mess like for this case. For them, it is like a regular thing, and certainly there is an escalation for your ticket to a VP, so yeah, that might be problematic for them.\" Eventually he and Colin landed on including the ticket number but not the individual names, since \"it was not like a kind of yanking me around, it was just kind of the way it is structured.\" This shows a diplomatic instinct that aligns with Colin's own coaching style from prior sets (architecture-diagrams-first, let smart people draw their own conclusions).\n\n### Saurav's architectural thinking maturing\n\nWhen the conversation pivoted to the Srinivas deliverable, Saurav independently proposed the same decoupled architecture pattern he had outlined on Apr 13 (Set 07): convert the current WebEx scraper from a bot to a service app, add an MCP layer, then a bot or downstream app on top. He extended the argument with a new and substantive detail on security: scrape once at space level and share the data across users rather than duplicating per-user, so that \"in case of any attack, so the blast radius is only that group.\" This is the first time any team member has framed the architecture in terms of blast-radius containment rather than just compute efficiency. Colin's response was to add security, privacy, and access control to the three-slide architecture framework Saurav is producing for Srinivas.\n\n### The \"do not tell the others\" aside\n\nColin was candid about the staffing rationale he had decided the day before in the Apr 14 EPNM one-on-one (Set 07a): he is moving Saurav toward cross-project work on EPNM while keeping Srikar and Namita CI/CD-only. He told Saurav directly: \"I do not want Srikar and Namita Vaishali to know that because they are going to say, hey, why am I not involved there? And I am like, because you guys have laptops number one and number two, I want you to keep going on here, especially the two that are in person.\" This is the first written record of the staffing split from Saurav's perspective and confirms the Apr 14 rule (Set 07a, item 2).\n\n## Meeting Context\n\n### Why this meeting happened\n\nThe meeting was originally scheduled as part of a regular one-on-one cadence between Colin and Saurav, but the Cisco laptop failure from the weekend (Saurav's Cisco MacBook broken after a macOS update) drove the agenda. Saurav had been in an active multi-day back-and-forth with Cisco IT support. On Apr 15 morning (Saurav's evening), Cisco IT communicated they would issue a loaner laptop but the primary repair would take one to two months.\n\n### The tax-day time constraint\n\nColin opened the meeting explicitly short on time because US tax filing deadline is the same day and his accountant appointment was approaching. The meeting had to cover the laptop escalation plan and kick off the Srinivas architecture deliverable strategy in 38 minutes. This compressed the architecture coaching into a high-signal block rather than a leisurely discussion. Colin also indicated he would miss most of the regular afternoon team session for the same reason, and asked Saurav to be his eyes and ears on that call.\n\n## New People Introduced\n\nNo new people introduced in this meeting.\n\n## External Parties Referenced\n\n- **Srinivas Pitta** (Cisco, DeepSight platform owner): referenced as the escalation target for Saurav's laptop issue and for the pending DeepSight access commitment from the Apr 10 call. Colin plans to message him directly today.\n- **Anand Singh** (Cisco, executive sponsor): referenced as the escalation target for the laptop issue. Colin expects Anand to \"make it right\" (prioritize new hardware, treat old hardware as file-recovery only).\n- **Imran** (Cisco stakeholder): referenced as present at the Apr 10 meeting. Saurav recalled Imran said \"bring this up with me at the start of the month\" as a deflection on DeepSight access.\n- **Justin Joseph** (Cisco build infrastructure): referenced in context of Colin's Apr 14 comment to him (\"what you put together already is a great foundation for us to build off of\"). Colin used this as a worked example of diplomatic framing for Cisco POC work.\n- **Naga (Nagabhushan)** (Cisco, Pulse and Scribbler owner): referenced as the still-pending contact for WebEx and Scribbler repo access.\n- **Srikar, Namita, Vaishali** (BayOne team): referenced by exclusion. Colin explicitly said he does not want them to know Saurav is being cross-staffed to EPNM.\n\n## Sentiment Notes\n\n- **Saurav's morale:** not deteriorated despite the laptop failure. He treated the data loss philosophically (\"I will just think of it as gone\"). No frustration expressed at the escalation plan. His engagement on the architecture discussion was as sharp as on Apr 13. The laptop blocker is an operational annoyance, not a motivation crisis.\n- **Colin's patience with Cisco IT:** thinning. Prior sets mentioned \"Cisco tribal knowledge\" as a neutral observation; in this meeting Colin explicitly called Cisco IT \"stupid\" (twice) and \"incompetent\" (once). This is strong language relative to his usual register and is directed at the IT function specifically, not at Srinivas or Anand.\n- **Colin's confidence in the renewal:** he referenced the renewal twice as leverage for the escalation but did not show anxiety about the renewal itself. Anand's Apr 16 extension posture (captured in org chart) appears to have survived into Apr 15 from Colin's perspective. The laptop issue is a quality-of-service complaint, not a relationship-endangering event.\n\n## Files in this set (planned)\n\n- `08_1on1_people_2026-04-15.md` — this file\n- `08_1on1_blockers_2026-04-15.md` — parallel agent, laptop saga plus downstream access blockers\n- `08_1on1_architecture_and_strategy_2026-04-15.md` — parallel agent, three-slide framework, Wispr fallback, Mermaid skill\n- `08_1on1_action_items_2026-04-15.md` — parallel agent, Saurav's email, Colin's escalation, DeepSight follow-up\n- `08_1on1_summary_2026-04-15.md` — last, main session\n- Bridge: `07a-08_changes_2026-04-15.md` — main session, chronological\n"}
```

That would be great, because we can use that for a ton of different things.\"\n   - Saurav extended this with security framing: scrape once at space level, share across users, so \"in case of any attack, the blast radius is only that group.\" First time blast-radius language appears in the research chain.\n\n3. **Saurav's self-service-architecture observation**\n   - Saurav: \"I got an idea of like why they are building like [Scribbler] and everything else like this kind of scripting. And what they are doing is they are literally just copying and pasting the code and calling it modular. That is not modular. That is technical debt that is spiraling that they are not even aware of.\"\n   - Vision: unified layers + single points of entry for access and maintenance; compatible with modular reusable design, not a monolith\n\n6. **Mermaid.js integration into Singularity skill**\n   - Colin just added Mermaid reference files to the Singularity skill (8 linked HTML files)\n   - Content: comprehensive library of Mermaid 11.2 capabilities — shapes, text formatting, Font Awesome and Feather icons, class definitions for reusable styling, subgraphs, diagram types (class, Gantt, pie charts, mind maps, milestone tracker, git graphs)\n   - Why it matters: Claude defaults to simplistic flow charts; these reference files enable richer architecture diagrams by pointing Claude to specific examples\n   - Known limitation: JavaScript padding issue on cut-off rendering (trivial fix)\n   - Saurav receives the 8 files via chat (URLs will be local paths, needs Claude to repath)\n\n7. **Critical assessment of current Cisco work**\n   - Pulse claim vs reality: Saurav's test — if Pulse is production-ready, they should have integrated data already in a DB; if so, why did they ask BayOne to build the scraper and the bot and the analysis?\n   - Implication: Pulse is likely also POC, not production\n   - Colin and Saurav agree: whatever Cisco says verbally is \"about seventy percent correct and thirty percent misremembered or misstated\"\n\n8. **Saurav's delivery responsibility while laptop is down**\n   - Colin: \"I'm going to shift a couple of responsibilities onto you that you can do without Cisco hardware\"\n   - Architecture work (documents and diagrams) is doable on BayOne hardware\n   - Saurav to lead the WebEx architecture deliverable for Friday\n   - Saurav has WebEx chat transcripts from prior work and can grab transcripts via the scraper he already built\n\n**Transcription corrections:**\n- \"Bay One\" / \"P1\" / \"B1\" = \"BayOne\"\n- \"Basel\" = \"Bazel\"\n- \"Wispr\" = \"Whisper\"\n- \"Deepak Site\" / \"deep site\" = \"DeepSight\"\n- \"Scribble\" / \"Scribbler\" = \"Scribbler\" (Naga's Whisper-based tool)\n- \"Pulse\" = Naga's WebEx scraping tool\n- \"Srinivas\" / \"Srini\" = \"Srinivas Pitta\"\n- \"Nxos\" / \"XY chat\" in context = \"NX-OS chat\"\n- \"Aditi\" in \"If it is that Aditi\" = transcription artifact;
